nativeRange.habitat: Shallow sandy and seagrass-bed substrates in tropical coastal waters; typically found with its foot clutching onto buried hard substrate or rock in sand flats and reef margins, from the intertidal zone to approximately 20 m depth

decorPreferences: deep sand bed (10+ cm) for foot burial
open sandy area with buried rock anchor at base
no corals or other anemones within 30 cm

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via symbiotic zooxanthellae; also an active predator. Generally advised to target-feed meaty marine foods — silversides, uncooked shrimp, chunks of fresh fish, krill, or large mysis shrimp — approximately once per week. Larger specimens may benefit from feeding every few days. Food should be placed directly onto the oral disc; tentacles will draw it in. Has been known to capture and consume small fish and invertebrates that wander into contact with its tentacles.

avoidWith: most fish not listed as natural clownfish hosts — high predation risk
small, nano, or slow-moving fish (blennies, gobies, dragonets, dartfish)
corals placed near the anemone's territory
invertebrates that may wander into contact with tentacles

summary: True Carpet Anemone (S. gigantea) is the largest carpet anemone — disc up to 91 cm — with extremely potent nematocysts. Iconic clownfish host; eats non-host fish. Advanced care; high captive mortality.
careNotes:
True Carpet Anemone (Stichodactyla gigantea) is widely considered the most demanding anemone in the reef hobby and is generally recommended only for experienced aquarists with established, stable reef systems. Its disc can reach up to 91 cm (3 feet) in diameter under ideal conditions; most captive specimens tend to reach 30–60 cm, with exceptional individuals exceeding 80 cm.

Key care requirements:

• Substrate: This species typically anchors its foot to a hard object (rock or rubble) buried beneath the sand, rather than burying directly into the sand column like S. haddoni. A sand bed of at least 10 cm is generally advised to allow the anemone to locate and grip buried substrate. Placement at the base of a large rock is strongly preferred.

• Lighting: High-intensity lighting is required to sustain its zooxanthellae. S. gigantea is widely considered more light-demanding than its sibling S. haddoni — generally advised PAR of 200–400 at the anemone's location is commonly cited in the hobby. Acclimate new specimens gradually by starting lower in the tank before moving to full-intensity zones.

• Flow: Moderate water movement is generally advised — sufficient to lightly ruffle the tentacle edges but not overwhelm the disc. Sources vary between 'moderate' and 'strong'; moderate is the more widely cited sweet spot. Powerhead intake guards on all pumps are essential, as wandering anemones can be pulled into powerheads with fatal results.

• Aggression and fish predation: This species has been documented capturing and consuming fish that make contact with its tentacles, including small reef fish, blennies, gobies, dragonets, and other bottom-dwelling or slow-moving species. Only its natural clownfish host species (listed in goodWith) tend to be safe, and even those may be at some risk if they are not an established symbiont species. Caution should be exercised with any tankmate not listed as a natural host.

• Clownfish hosting: S. gigantea is one of the iconic host anemones for Amphiprion ocellaris and A. percula (the two most popular clownfish in the hobby). It also naturally hosts A. perideraion, A. clarkii, A. akindynos, A. bicinctus, A. rubrocinctus, and A. barberi in the wild. Captive clownfish will often adopt this anemone enthusiastically.

• Human handling: The nematocysts of S. gigantea are extremely potent — described as 'extremely sticky to the touch' with firm tentacle adhesion. The sting has been documented causing severe localized reactions, especially on sensitive skin. Thick gloves and long-sleeved protection are strongly advised when working in a tank housing this species. The sting has no aquarium antidote; treatment is symptomatic.

• Water quality and stability: Requires a well-established reef system (6+ months minimum). Temperature, salinity, and pH should remain highly stable — even minor swings have been linked to anemone bleaching and death. Alkalinity (dKH) is generally advised in the 8–12 range; calcium 380–450 ppm and magnesium 1250–1400 ppm are standard reef values.

• Acclimation: S. gigantea is notably sensitive to shipping stress and should be drip-acclimated over at least 1–2 hours before introduction. New specimens may not fully open for several days; a partially-deflated or wandering anemone immediately after introduction is common. Quarantine and prophylactic treatment for bacterial infections is sometimes recommended by experienced keepers, as shipping stress frequently predisposes specimens to bacterial lesions.

• Captive mortality: This species has a reputation for high captive mortality, with hobbyist accounts suggesting many specimens do not survive beyond 3–5 years in captivity. This is attributed to shipping stress, inability to meet its intense lighting demand, and parameter instability.

• Size vs. Haddon's: S. gigantea tends to have slightly smaller overall disc size than S. haddoni (which reaches 50–91 cm) but has notably more potent and sticky tentacles. Its tentacles are shorter and vibrate constantly, distinguishing it visually from S. haddoni's longer, less-sticky tentacles.

• Temperature note: LiveAquaria lists 72–78°F (22–26°C) while AquariumBreeder and other specialist sources list 25–28°C; the 24–27°C range here represents the cross-source average. Standard reef temperature at the higher end of that band (26–27°C) is likely more natural for this Indo-Pacific shallow-water species.

• Tank size note: LiveAquaria and AquariumBreeder both list 50 gallons (189–200 L) as the minimum; specialist reef sources and the adult size potential (up to 91 cm) suggest this is insufficient for long-term adult keeping. 300 L (79 US gallons) is adopted here as a more conservative minimum that provides adequate footprint; experienced keepers commonly recommend 400–600 L.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of Stichodactyla gigantea has not been reported in the hobby literature. In the wild, reproduction occurs via both sexual (broadcast spawning) and asexual (pedal laceration) methods. Pedal laceration — where small portions of the foot break off and develop into new individuals — is occasionally observed in captivity, but intentional propagation is not practiced in the hobby.

additional.name: LiveAquaria – True Carpet Anemone, Brown (product variant cross-check)
additional.notes: Cross-checked all water parameters, care level, feeding, and hosted clownfish species. Confirmed temperature 72–78°F, SG 1.020–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, care level Expert Only, min tank 50 gal, lighting High, flow Strong. Clownfish hosts confirmed as 8 species including ocellaris, percula, clarkii, perideraion, akindynos, rubrocinctus, barberi, bicinctus.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Stichodactyla gigantea
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Family: Stichodactylidae, Order: Actiniaria), native range (Indo-Pacific including Hawaii, Red Sea), adult size (typically 50 cm, max 80 cm oral disc), habitat description (seagrass beds and sand flats), and clownfish hosting list. Size discrepancy: Wikipedia gives max 80 cm; LiveAquaria gives up to 91 cm (36 in). Both values considered; 91 cm retained as maximum since hobbyist data reflects extended captive growth under ideal conditions.

additional.name: ReefAquarium.com – Gigantea Carpet Anemone
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (34–36 inches / 86–91 cm), substrate requirements (approx. 4 inches / ~10 cm sand depth), flow (low to moderate), lighting (high), care level (Difficult), and feeding notes. Identified minimum tank size as 75 gal (284 L) — higher than LiveAquaria's 50 gal; 300 L selected as rounded conservative value bridging both recommendations.

additional.name: AquariumBreeder – Proper Care for Carpet Anemones
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters specific to S. gigantea: temperature 25–28°C, SG 1.023–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, native range (Micronesia to Red Sea, Australia to Ryukyu Islands), depth range 1–20 m, adult size up to 50 cm. Temperature range differs from LiveAquaria (22–26°C vs 25–28°C); 24–27°C used as cross-source average.
